data.title: BridgeBio: Attruby's Early Dominance Makes The Bull Case Hard To Ignore
data.description:
Overview

During my last look in 2024, I rated BridgeBio Pharma, Inc. (BBIO) a “Buy,” but expressed some concern over competition and a limited cash runway. Pfizer (PFE) was already dominant in the ATTR-CM market with products like Vyndamax. And I thought that BridgeBio could benefit from an EU partnership or some sort of non-dilutive capital.

Be it from shuffling debt around or through the monetization of acoramidis in the EU via a royalty sale, BridgeBio has "kept the lights on." Acoramidis has also since been approved in ATTR-CM as “Attruby” in the U.S., and Pfizer is fighting a few generic drug makers who filed ANDAs challenging Vyndamax (tafamidis) patents.

The situation is a bit complicated for BridgeBio because although it competes head-on with Vyndamax, if tafamidis generics enter the ATTR-CM market earlier than hoped, it would weaken the Vyndamax/Attruby TTR stabilizer “duopoly” and lead to price erosion.

In the article below, I propose that positive Attruby-related developments, alongside pipeline advancements, merit ongoing optimism in BridgeBio's stock despite its premium valuation amid ongoing losses and debt obligations.

Attruby

Pfizer just settled three major cases, with a negotiated generic entry date of June 1, 2031 (one, Apotex Corp., is outstanding). This is extended from what had previously been an anticipated 2029 cliff. This is “gospel" to the ears of BridgeBio and its investors because the ATTR-CM market remains a "protected branded space" through at least mid-2031, giving Attruby more time to establish its roots.

Already bearing fruit, Attruby generated $362.4 million in U.S. net product revenues last year. And BridgeBio estimates that Attruby accounts for over 25% of new-to-brand prescriptions.

Now, both BridgeBio’s and Pfizer's drugs are TTR stabilizers. Attruby was designed to have much better potency. BridgeBio has run with this differentiation in marketing language, citing Attruby as the “first and only approved product with a label specifying near-complete stabilization of TTR.” Since there are no head-to-head trials comparing the two, however, any "superiority" claim rests on cross-trial comparisons, which may not resonate with prescribers.

I did a DCF model on Attruby.

My assumptions include an 11% discount rate, or WACC. I have the drug peaking in 2031, with its loss of exclusivity year slated for 2039. In its recent annual filing, BridgeBio cited a target population of 50,000 in the U.S. Now, ATTR-CM is famously underdiagnosed, so I accounted for 3% annual population growth as diagnosis improves. Obviously, peak share assumptions are up for much debate, but I thought 18% was appropriate. It’s strong but also accounts for the "second-mover disadvantage." A $200,000 net price per year is 20% gross-to-net. COGS is at 8%. Launch opex is 50% for three years before settling at 30% steady-state opex. And, beginning in 2032, when tafamidis generics are expected to enter the market, I modeled for a 15% competitive impact (impacting market share assumptions).

I have Attruby generating just over $2.2 billion in peak annual sales, worth $5.46 billion in net present value. My estimate is on the conservative end of analyst estimates. If you do a Google search, you'll see that most estimates fall between $2 billion and $5 billion in peak annual sales. And although I lean conservative, as I usually do, I can certainly see the case for up to $4 billion in peak annual sales, which is why I also provided a sensitivity analysis in the image above.

For instance, if you bump up peak share assumptions to 22%, then the drug peaks at roughly $2.7 billion. Likewise, if I understated the annual population growth and it is, say, 5% instead, Attruby would peak near $3.5 billion. So, it goes to show that even small changes in key assumptions can have large impacts.

In any event, BridgeBio has an enterprise value near $15 billion, so this leads me to its pipeline in search of residual value.

Pipeline

Management likes to highlight three late-stage assets that could each represent over $1 billion in peak U.S. sales opportunities.

Infigratinib is differentiated because it is a once-daily pill. The market leader, BioMarin's (BMRN) Voxzogo, is a daily subcutaneous injection. But I think that a once-daily pill is a big competitive advantage in a pediatric population. Moreover, BridgeBio's PROPEL 3 study, which had data released in February, infigratinib showed a mean height velocity increase of plus 2.1 centimeters per year and also achieved the first statistically significant improvement in body proportionality, or the ratio of the upper body to the lower body. Mind you, Voxzogo nearly eclipsed $1 billion in sales last year.

Then we have encaleret. This one works by antagonizing calcium-sensing receptors to counteract the “gain-of-function” mutation in ADH1 patients that tells the body their calcium levels are high when they are actually low. Encaleret generated positive Phase 3 data in October: 76% of encaleret patients achieved target blood and urine calcium levels compared to just 4% on conventional therapy (i.e., high-dose calcium and Vitamin D). As BridgeBio’s patient estimates imply, this is a tiny market, though (25k US + EU). Encaleret's biggest opportunity is in chronic hypoparathyroidism (awaiting a Phase 2 trial initiation).

Finally, BBP-418 is positioned to become the first approved therapy for any form of limb-girdle muscular dystrophy after Phase 3 FORTIFY interim data earlier this year showed a rapid treatment effect. Patients completed a 100-meter walk 31 seconds faster than the placebo group after 12 months, and there were significant reductions seen in creatine kinase.

Financial Health

BridgeBio generated $502.1 million in revenue in 2025. About 25% of that came from "license and services" revenue. The company spent ~$452 million on R&D and ~$531 million on SG&A. Loss from operations last year was ~$523 million. The company ended 2025 with ~$587 million in cash and securities.

BridgeBio owed $855 million in deferred royalty obligations, $547 million in 2027 Notes, $741 million in 2029 Notes, and $565 million in 2031 Notes.

In January, management completed the issuance of $632.5 million aggregate principal amount of 2033 convertible Notes. This should cover the Notes due 2027.

Assuming similar cash burn, this gives them roughly two years of cash runway. From looking at consensus EPS and revenue estimates, BridgeBio is expected to become "EPS positive" in 2027.

Conclusion

Attruby is off to a rock-solid start in ATTR-CM, and Pfizer developments are bullish. Attruby should generate anywhere between $2 and $4 billion in peak annual revenue. In the interim, BridgeBio’s pipeline is compelling. I imagine there is at least another blockbuster or two in the near future. BridgeBio’s losses and debt obligations are worth watching, but I’m not too concerned. I think Attruby alone will power them to cash generation and allow them to deleverage (without sacrificing their “R&D engine”) within two to three years. So, I still see BBIO as a “Buy,” even near all-time highs.

Some risks to my “Buy” rating include regulatory setbacks (i.e., NDA rejections, label restrictions), competition in ATTR-CM (i.e., "second-mover disadvantage," GTN squeeze, Amvuttra, next-gen nucresiran, NTLA-2001) and achondroplasia (Ascendis's once-weekly injection, Yuviwel), clinical trial failures, operational overload (from potentially launching three new drugs within an 18-month window), and a shareholder-unfriendly capital structure (i.e., outstanding debt and royalty obligations).

data.title: CSRE: The Secret Sauce Of This Outperforming Active REIT ETF
data.description:
"Real estate remains one of the few areas in global capital markets where active management reliably creates value." --Sam Sahn, Portfolio Manager at Hazelview Investments

I've invested in real estate investment trusts ("REITs") since 2013, when my dad prodded me to pick a few stocks to buy in an attempt to help me learn about investing. I wasn't interested in investing at the time but bought a few stocks anyway. Two of those were REITs.

Years later, when I opened my brokerage account again, I was flabbergasted at what I saw.

My handful of stocks were not only higher in market price, they were also generating a steady stream of dividend income.

I understood that's how it works, in theory. But seeing it in real life was amazing. I've been an active investor of REITs ever since.

Lots of investors recognize the value of an allocation to REITs but lack the interest or time to be an active REIT-picker themselves.

That's where actively managed REIT ETFs come in.

A surprising number of active REIT ETFs have been launched in recent years. Among the best of those is the Cohen & Steers Real Estate Active ETF (CSRE), launched in early 2025.

Since inception, CSRE is virtually tied with the Janus Henderson US Real Estate ETF (JRE) for best performing active REIT ETF. Here are the two compared to four other actively managed REIT ETF peers:

  • Invesco Active US Real Estate ETF (PSR)
  • DFA Dimensional US Real Estate ETF (DFAR)
  • JPMorgan Realty Income ETF (JPRE)
  • ALPS Active REIT ETF (REIT)

CSRE has spent most of its existence as a publicly traded ETF outperforming its peers, but JRE has pulled ahead recently after a strong start to the year.

Interestingly, all 6 of these ETFs have outperformed the passively managed Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ) by anywhere from 1 to 5 percentage points since early 2025. Here's just the top two vs. VNQ for the sake of a cleaner illustration:

What's the secret sauce of these two actively managed REIT ETFs, especially CSRE?

Let's dig in to answer that question. Afterward, I'll discuss who active REIT ETFs might be right for and why I don't personally use them.

The Case For REITs

First, let's explain the bull case for commercial real estate in general. REITs tend to perform well when their underlying commercial real estate assets perform well.

In fact, REIT stock prices tend to move in advance of commercial real estate prices. And commercial real estate prices are heavily affected by long-term interest rates. While long-term interest rates have recently spiked due to the Iran war and high oil prices, the longer term trajectory still appears to be downward.

As such, while it could be delayed by the Iran war, commercial real estate broadly does seem to be entering a multi-year rebound into price appreciation.

Of course, "commercial real estate" is a very broad and diverse umbrella of various property types. The trajectory of multifamily may look very different than the trajectory of data centers.

But, generally speaking, the bulk of the CRE market moves in tandem with long-term interest rates.

Another bullish point supporting the CRE rebound is the supply outlook.

Across almost all sub-sectors, the development pipeline has shrunk considerably as a result of elevated capital and construction costs.

Due to tariffs on construction inputs as well as deportations that disproportionately impact the construction industry, construction costs continue to rise at a rapid pace.

This raises the hurdle rate to greenlight new CRE projects, and it also increases the gap between existing property values and replacement costs. Both are bullish for existing CRE properties.

Now, moving on to the bull case for publicly traded REITs.

If CRE enjoys a new multi-year trajectory of price appreciation, then so should REITs.

Plus, REITs on the whole continue to trade at a material discount to the broader stock market, although the valuation gap has narrowed somewhat this year.

While the average REIT discount to the broader stock market is about 0.6x, the current discount is about 3.6x.

Historically, when REITs have traded at such a considerable discount to the broader market, forward 1-year and 2-year returns have exceeded those of the broader stock market.

While it is impossible to say if past performance will translate into future performance, it is enough to say that the record of history is on REITs' side right now.

Active Outperformance Vs. Higher Fees

According to research from Hazelview Investments, actively managed REIT portfolios outperformed their passive index peers over a 15-year period of time, delivering 151 basis points of annual outperformance.

Additionally, the research found that actively managed strategies boasted superior risk-adjusted returns than passive strategies while achieving greater diversification across sectors and market cap sizes.

While passive real estate indices are heavily concentrated in large-cap, high-multiple REITs in the data center, senior housing, and industrial sub-sectors, actively managed ETFs diversify more heavily across real estate sub-sectors and market caps.

More so than the broader stock market, heavy indexing in the real estate sector has led to material inefficiencies that active managers can exploit.

Moreover, generalist stock investors tend to lack expertise in commercial real estate, while most experts in commercial real estate work in... commercial real estate. Relatively few investment professionals have deep proficiency in both commercial real estate and stock investing. That renders further inefficiencies in the realm of REITs.

As of 2024, actively managed real estate strategies outperformed passive real estate indices by 1-2% per year.

Compare that to most other actively managed stock funds, especially mid-cap and large-cap, that actually underperformed their passive benchmarks.

This is sobering data for the average stock-picker. If even most professional investors cannot outperform their passive index benchmarks, how likely is it that a retail investor will outperform a passive stock fund?

But the publicly traded real estate sector is a different story. Here, active managers consistently outperform.

Why?

The reasons given above play a big role. But also consider that Wall Street devotes fewer analysts and less resources to REIT coverage than for most other stocks.

That results in a wider dispersion between analyst consensus earnings forecasts and actual earnings results for REITs than for non-REIT stocks. (More analyst coverage generally renders a more accurate analyst consensus estimate.)

This, again, results in greater potential for mispricing of REITs than of the average non-REIT stock.

That gives active managers who specialize in commercial real estate and are skilled at fundamental analysis of REITs an advantage.

However, active REIT funds and ETFs also sport higher expense ratios and lower dividend yields than their passive peers.

Here's a quick comparison of VNQ to the 6 actively managed ETFs listed above:

ETF Expense Ratio 30-Day SEC Yield
VNQ 0.13% 3.6%
CSRE 0.70% 1.9%
JRE 0.65% 2.5%
PSR 0.35% 3.3%
DFAR 0.19% 2.7%
JPRE 0.50% 3.2%
REIT 0.68% 3.2%

VNQ has the highest yield and lowest expense ratio.

CSRE has the lowest yield and highest expense ratio.

Interestingly, the two best performing active ETFs since CSRE's inception also have some of the highest expense ratios.

So far, they have earned their higher fees through outperformance over VNQ, but it has come at the cost of a lower dividend yield. I am guessing that this is due to their exclusion of high-yielding value traps in the REIT space as well as their focus on the fastest growing REITs.

Still, that does diminish a lot of the appeal of owning REITs, which are designed to be income machines (though aren't always managed that way).

Making this an even tougher sell, REIT dividends are largely non-qualified, meaning that they are taxed at a generally higher rate than the mostly qualified dividends of non-REIT stocks.

Worse still, these actively managed REIT ETFs don't even outperform higher-yielding and lower-fee qualified dividend ETFs like the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD):

At least, they haven't outperformed SCHD for as long as they have existed as publicly traded vehicles.

But perhaps that will change in the coming years because of the bull case for commercial real estate and REITs outlined above.

CSRE's Secret Sauce

The first to note about CSRE is who manages it.

CSRE is issued by Cohen & Steers, which specializes in commercial real estate and other hard assets like infrastructure. C&S is the largest active REIT manager by assets under management.

Generally speaking, it would be sensible to opt for a management company/sponsor that specializes in a given space rather than one with no particular specialty.

Also note that CSRE is managed primarily by Jason Yablon, Head of Listed Real Estate at Cohen & Steers, who has analyzed REITs his entire career.

Of course, experience does not necessarily equate to skill.

So let's look at another Cohen & Steers fund for which Yablon is a portfolio manager: The Cohen & Steers Real Estate Securities Fund (CSDIX):

Sure enough, this mutual fund from C&S has outperformed the passive index, especially over the last decade.

And due to lower fees, CSRE has slightly outperformed CSDIX during its brief lifespan thus far.

As far as I can tell, Cohen & Steers imposes few rules on the managers of CSRE.

The team basically engages in bottom-up fundamental analysis to boil the roughly 150-stock investable universe down to their highest conviction picks.

As of the end of Q1 2026, that was 40 REITs. But even within this 40-REIT portfolio, the managers heavily concentrate the fund into their highest conviction picks. The top 10 holdings account for 58% of the portfolio.

While the passive, cap-weighted index allocates a ~10% weighting to leading senior housing REIT Welltower (WELL), CSRE allocates a roughly 50% greater weight to the REIT.

That's a common theme among actively managed REIT ETFs. JRE sports a ~12% allocation to WELL.

It's also clear that CSRE's managers are very bullish on technology-related real estate such as data centers and wireless communication towers, as these two sub-sectors together make up ~30% of the fund.

Other than what's stated above, it is hard to find more to say about this actively managed fund, because it isn't rules-based. It's up to the discretion of the portfolio managers.

Bottom Line

For investors who primarily aim for total returns (as opposed to income) and want to be as hands-off as possible, CSRE makes a fine option. In fact, it makes a strong argument for being the best option for exposure to REITs.

But the downsides of CSRE are hard to ignore. It has not outperformed most qualified dividend ETFs during its brief lifespan, and it offers a lower yield than most of those non-REIT ETFs as well.

Moreover, the 0.70% expense ratio will be a tough pill to swallow for many investors. A $10,000 investment in CSRE would cost $70 per year in fees. A $100,000 investment would cost $700 per year. Investors with a sizable nest egg can see those fees stack up quickly.

At High Yield Landlord, we seek to fill this gap by providing real-money, actively managed REIT portfolios overseen by managers with backgrounds in commercial real estate. And best of all, we don't sacrifice either quality or yield in our portfolio construction.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Meta Q1: Thanks For The Dip, I'm Buying More
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) was one of the most recent positions I've added to my portfolio (check it here), and I am satisfied with my decision so far. Even with a lot of news flow (check it here), both positive and negative, since the beginning of my position, my conviction in the long-term thesis has become even stronger.

After Q1 earnings, this became even more evident; I believe the market is still overlooking some positive points about Meta. I found the result solid, and even so the stock is falling in after-hours. The valuation was already good; with this decline, the margin of safety becomes even more obvious.

Meta’s Q1 Earnings

Let’s address the elephant in the room: Meta’s EPS was excellent and beat all estimates. We are talking about a $10.44 EPS in a single quarter, 62% growth. The market expected something closer to ~$6.6, so this was a huge surprise.

But getting straight to the point, this was non-recurring; it includes a huge (~$8 billion) tax benefit that made the effective tax rate -23%. So basically, it is not worth becoming optimistic based on this earnings beat.

However, I think it is possible to be optimistic about the rest of the indicators. Revenue grew an impressive 33% YoY, and this was also a beat by $760 million. With this very strong top-line momentum, operating income also performed well, growing 30%.

So we are still talking about a high-growth company, even ignoring the inflated EPS.

What makes me most optimistic is how Meta delivered this ~33% YoY revenue growth. YoY the number of users increased 4%, even with headwinds from Iran and Russia. Ad impressions increased a lot, growing 19%, and along with that, price per ad grew 12%. This creates an absurd compounding effect for the top line. We are talking about high ad growth, but revenue per ad also grew significantly, two components forming revenue that are in a very strong momentum, and that is why revenue is growing so much.

For me, this only reinforces that Meta’s core remains strong. I have had some concerns regarding the sustainability of continuing to increase users, or even continuing to increase ad impressions, since most of Meta’s platforms are already very mature. But Meta continues to prove that it can improve monetizable space, and that its initiatives allow its apps to become more profitable and also maintain or increase engagement levels.

One point I found more negative was margin. My expectation is that in the long term, Meta will be able to become more efficient and expand margin, even if gradually, through automation, pricing power, scale, and so on. But in this quarter, we saw that it is not delivering exactly this. But I do not see this as a problem because, besides the margin remaining flat, the biggest growth came from R&D expenses, which grew 46%. Whether we like it or not, this is the type of expense that allows revenue to continue growing, directly or indirectly.

Market Did Not Like Meta’s CapEx

Especially in the first hours, it is difficult to be certain about the reason for the market’s reaction. Maybe some funds/algorithms did not like the operating margin, or maybe they did not like the guidance for the next quarter. But I believe the two main factors, and maybe my opinion will change after fully listening to the earnings call, is that the market did not like CapEx.

In Q1, CapEx was $19.8 billion, below income from operations (although still a high number). But I believe the real issue is that guidance was increased once again. The company stated that now in 2026, capital expenditures should be in the range of $125 billion to $145 billion (previously the range was $115-$135 billion). So it was already at a high level, the market already did not like it, and now this expectation became even worse.

Indeed, for a company with income from operations around $22 billion, that is, something close to $90 billion annually, saying that capital expenditure can be $145 billion in one year is a lot. We should see strong pressure on free cash flow, possibly negative margins, increasing depreciation in the next quarters, possibly some level of debt or reduction in dividend/buyback payments. Basically, CapEx directly influences several numbers and expectations the market may have.

But I believe the uncertainty stops there; it is not something that affects the long-term thesis that much. Of course, if all this CapEx turns out to be useless, we would be talking about a horrible scenario where Meta could have distributed billions and billions to shareholders but burned it on initiatives that failed. But that would be the extreme scenario, perhaps worse than the Metaverse.

In practice, I believe many positive things will materialize from this infrastructure investment. I do not know what the ROIC will be, but I believe that in the long term, Meta will be able to extract positive growth to justify this type of investment, whether through new features, new revenues, or at least consolidating its platforms even further to maintain leadership in its core market.

Meta: Valuation And The Long-Term

Quarters are important both to see how the company is performing (beats and misses) and also to increase or decrease our long-term conviction. In this case, I believe Q1 earnings strongly reinforced how positive Meta’s long-term will be. As I mentioned in the first section, ad impressions and price per ad are Meta’s core, and the reported numbers were solid. Thinking exclusively about these two metrics, how will they look in 10 years?

Personally, I am optimistic. With so much investment in AI and mainly advances in this area, I believe Meta will continue to be able to create platforms with even higher engagement. For example, with AI, it is possible to further improve algorithms to deliver what people like, and it is also possible to encourage people to create more content and more quickly. This type of technology also accelerates new features for the platform. Threads is not showing strong engagement recently. Easier to analyze and easier to fix. All of this results in more people using and more people seeing ads.

The price per ad is also a trend that has been happening for some time. The better the algorithm, the better Meta becomes at delivering the right ad to the right person, in the right way, at the right time, and the more advertisers can pay. It is a win-win situation.

But beyond that, right on the first page of the earnings release, Zuckerberg highlighted the “first model from Meta Superintelligence Labs” and also mentioned that it is “on track to deliver personal superintelligence to billions of people.” I believe this creates an enormous level of optionality, and much of this comes from the massive investments Meta is making, which is precisely a part the market does not like very much.

Maybe you do not trust the business model of providing LLMs to consumers and charging a subscription for it, and indeed, it is not an easy business; CapEx is high, and competitors are very aggressive - Alphabet (GOOGL, GOOG), OpenAI (OPENAI), and Anthropic (ANTHRO). But Meta does not need to play exactly the same game. It already has strong distribution. If it can add more features to WhatsApp, which is used by billions of users, such as some AI to support WhatsApp business, that is already a large addressable market. If it can provide some service for content creators, that is another large addressable market. If this AI (such as Muse Spark) drives sales of Meta Glasses, that is another addressable market that is not only large but is also practically being created. So even if it is not possible to state that AI will generate $X billions of revenue for Meta by 2030, this type of investment creates so many possibilities that it makes me more optimistic and more convinced that in a few years Meta will have higher revenue, maintain strong growth, and also have more diversified revenue.

Regarding valuation, this is where Meta makes even more sense. After the ~6% decline, Meta’s market cap is $1.6 trillion. It seems high, right? But Meta just confirmed that it is a company capable of delivering $88 billion of operating income annually, disregarding growth. Just with these numbers, we can say there is a price-to-OI of ~18x currently.

But the point that really matters is growth. With this already low multiple and with such high growth, there is very attractive compounding. The market is estimating an EPS close to $36 for Meta in 2027. The P/E is 17x. If Meta can deliver 20% EPS growth per year until 2030, in a few years the P/E would drop to 10x (EPS would go to $62). 20% per year is a lot; it is not a base case assumption, but with so much investment, so much optionality, and a core with solid momentum, it is possible.

In this scenario not only shareholder yield would be high, but also the implicit upside. For instance, to be traded at only ~20x forward earnings, by 2029 we would need to see a 100% upside (with Meta stock at $1,244).

Bottom Line

Meta is not one of those cases where it is possible to have complete certainty. There are many regulatory risks that could cause eventual fines or even some loss of moat. There are also macroeconomic risks and/or changes in consumer preference that affect prospects for ad price growth and ad impressions. But even considering this, I think the market is underestimating Meta’s potential.

Uncertainty exists, but Meta’s moats also exist. Execution is good, and numbers are strong. All of this makes it possible to trust the long-term, and the good part is that since the scenario is not crystal clear, the valuation adjusts to that and offers an interesting margin of safety. Therefore, I maintain my Buy rating and I plan to increase my (small) position a little more at these prices.

data.title: ServiceNow: The AI Control Tower The Market Is Ignoring
data.description:
Software season is back with uncertainty as high as ever. ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW) has struggled in spite of delivering strong numbers, clear evidence of the poor sentiment in the software sector. Investors appear eager to dismiss near-term numbers due to fears regarding long-term AI disruption. I believe that this narrative underestimates the resiliency and importance of NOW’s platform as well as the company’s potential to offset that same narrative with efficiencies of its own. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.

NOW Stock Price

I last covered NOW in March, where I upgraded the stock to a strong buy rating amidst the SaaS apocalypse. The stock has fallen another 25% since.

The numbers paint a very different picture than that implied by the stock price. The company is executing on its AI opportunity, and the market continues to sleep.

NOW Stock Key Metrics

NOW is an enterprise software company that is famous for enabling digital workflows. Because workflows span vast use cases and the entirety of the business lifecycle, NOW has historically benefitted from a large and growing market.

That has culminated in strong financial results, with NOW being one of the rare software companies able to sustain aggressive top-line growth at scale. In the most recent quarter, NOW generated 22% YoY subscription revenue growth to $3.67 billion, slightly exceeding guidance of $3.653 billion. That beat, however, was rather small, and I suspect some investors may have interpreted this as a potential canary in the coal mine.

The company delivered solid growth in current remaining performance obligations (‘cRPOs’), which jumped 22.5% YoY. This, however, was slower than the 25% growth rate in the fourth quarter. I note that in my experience, cRPO growth has rarely correlated with forward revenue growth in spite of many apparently viewing it to be a reliable predictor of it. I suspect that some investors may have been hoping for an acceleration in both revenue and cRPO growth but were disappointed when they received neither.

NOW generated a solid 97% gross renewal rate in the quarter. This is a testament to how sticky the business is, indicating the minimal churn among customers (100% is the maximum result for this metric). Investors are concerned that this renewal rate may eventually fall if customers either replace enterprise solutions with cheaper alternatives (I view this to be an unlikely threat) or if customers use AI to reduce seats (this appears to be the primary driver behind the headwinds in the software sector). This will definitely be a critical metric to monitor to see if churn rises due to AI.

NOW did see its subscription gross margins decline roughly 300 bps in the quarter. Investors may be concerned that implementing AI may reduce the long-term profitability of the business.

Even so, NOW managed to post GAAP profits while also boosting non-GAAP operating margins by 100 bps to 32%, exceeding guidance of 31.5%. I expect profitability to be the main driving force behind any future software recovery.

It is curious to note that in spite of the bearishness surrounding potential AI disruption to its business, NOW has continued to increase its headcount. This appears consistent with management’s view that AI is a major tailwind for their business and an opportunity worth investing in.

NOW ended the quarter with $7.9 billion of cash and $1.7 billion of strategic investments versus $1.5 billion of debt, representing a strong net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to see around 22.5% YoY subscription revenue growth in the second quarter. Management slightly increased the full-year guide to see up to $15.775 billion in revenue, representing 22.5% YoY growth and up from the prior guide for $15.50 billion.

On the conference call, management noted that “50% of net new business now comes from a non-seat-based pricing model.” I expect this to be a high watermark among seat-based software peers. It is impressive that NOW has been able to shift towards usage-based pricing in such a dramatic way without it visibly affecting overall growth rates. Management did note that the conflict in Iran had some “potential impact to deal timing,” which appeared to have taken some investors by surprise. Nonetheless, management continues to view the company as being the “AI control tower” for its customers. The way I look at it, NOW has an opportunity to be the very thing that investors are afraid AI might do to it: be the orchestration layer for AI agents to implement work on other software solutions. Time will tell if management is able to rise up to the challenge.

Is NOW Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

There once was a time (technically less than a year ago) when NOW commanded healthy, premium valuations. Those days are long gone. While NOW still commands a premium relative to other software names, a lot of that looks justified given the company’s mission-critical positioning within its customer base. This is the kind of software that is central to business functioning and productivity. What doesn’t make a lot of sense is the stock valuation relative to growth rates, with the stock recently trading at just around 22x this year’s earnings.

Consensus estimates call for the company to finally see top-line growth decelerate below the magical 20% range over the coming years.

In my view, consensus estimates for profitability look quite understated. For instance, analysts expect NOW to generate around a 26.8% net margin by 2032. That would represent minimal margin expansion from the 26.2% estimate for this year. Sure, the company did indeed generate a paltry 18% GAAP incremental operating margin in the most recent quarter.

That said, I expect the company to post dramatically higher net margins over the long term, more in the 40% to 50% range. This would be due to AI-led efficiencies, which ironically form the backbone of the bear thesis. While bearish investors are concerned that NOW’s customers might use AI to reduce headcount, thus impacting seat-based pricing models, that might very well present the opportunity. Management appears focused on the long-term growth story, which of course remains important; however, for investors buying at these valuations, profitability appears to be the most visible catalyst. If we assume that NOW can generate a 40% net margin over the long term, then the stock is trading at around 14.5x long-term earnings. That multiple looks too conservative given that the top line is expected to grow at a healthy double-digit pace for a long time, alongside this remaining a highly resilient business. Perhaps the reader might wonder why I am so confident in a 40% net margin (or even higher). I note that mature companies like Adobe (ADBE) and Microsoft (MSFT) are already generating GAAP margins in the 30% to 40% range, and this is without the full force of AI. The prevailing bearish thesis centers around companies being able to replace human labor with AI agents. But software companies like NOW, in theory, would be able to undergo any such replacement with even greater intensity, given that these companies tend to have high fixed costs that appear to be well in the crosshairs of AI.

On the other hand, I expect NOW’s software to prove more resilient than peers, as I am doubtful that customers will be in a rush to replace the digital workflow provider, especially given that the company is investing aggressively to be a leader. These point to the potential for some multiple expansion - I see up to 50% potential upside if the stock trades up to a still-conservative 1.5x PEG ratio. If management delivers on their goals of becoming the AI control tower and delivers stronger growth than expected, then the upside may be even higher, but that is not needed at current valuations.

NOW Stock Risks

It is possible that NOW gets disrupted by AI. This might occur due to more drastic cuts to customer seats or perhaps AI enabling competitors to encroach on its market. It is possible that NOW engages in expensive M&A in efforts to change the narrative. It is possible that NOW loses its premium relative to other seat-based software vendors. It is possible that profit margins do not expand as expected, which might occur if NOW needs to lower prices to retain business.

NOW Stock Conclusion

I really do think that in due time, we will look back at this period with the understanding that while AI has indeed transformed how we work, the bearishness in software was dramatically overstated. NOW looks like a clear buying opportunity as it is successfully balancing between aggressively investing in its platform, delivering top-line growth, and expanding profit margins. The stock valuation is undemanding and offers significant upside potential. NOW is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my strong buy rating for the stock.

data.title: April FOMC: Hawks Push Back As Inflation Risks Rise
data.description:
The April FOMC meeting delivered a familiar outcome on the surface, with the Federal Reserve opting to hold rates steady for a third consecutive meeting, but the underlying message was far more nuanced. A shift in the Fed’s inflation assessment, combined with rising energy prices and an unusual number of dissents, revealed a Committee that is becoming less comfortable with the idea that easing is the natural next step. While the official stance remains unchanged, the tone of the statement and the discussion around it suggest that the path forward is becoming more uncertain, with risks to inflation once again taking center stage. Notably, these dynamics stand in contrast to the more dovish implications often associated with the incoming Fed Chair, Kevin Warsh, highlighting that the broader Committee may not be aligned with an easier policy path.

Decision

The Federal Reserve has once again opted to leave the target range of the federal funds rate unchanged at 3.50% to 3.75% at the conclusion of the April 2026 FOMC meeting. This is the third consecutive pause following the 75 bps worth of cuts that occurred across the final three meetings in 2025. And for a second time, the decision to keep rates unchanged was nearly unanimous except for one dissent, Governor Miran preferring a quarter-point cut.

However, that was not the only dissent. Today’s announcement noted the dissent of three of the presidents of the regional Federal Reserve branches, Beth Hammack (FRB of Cleveland), Neel Kashkari (FRB of Minneapolis), and Lorie Logan (FRB of Dallas), who “did not support inclusion of an easing bias in the statement at this time.” This objection seems to refer specifically to the statement “In considering the extent and timing of additional adjustments…” which suggests the next rate move will be a cut. The message here is clear: these three members of the voting committee did not think it was appropriate to be thinking about cuts when the upside risks to inflation have increased significantly since the mid-March meeting (which took place March 17-18th).

Press Release

Let’s break down the press release further. Beyond the new dissents, there were a few changes to the opening paragraphs describing economic conditions.

  • There were a few changes to the statements describing economic activity and employment, but nothing too groundbreaking. The addition of “on average” in the description of low job gains confirms that the Fed is looking through the volatility in the jobs numbers to the broader trend (March job gains were 178k, but the 3-month average is 68k).
  • The most significant change in the first paragraph is in the description of inflation from being “somewhat elevated” to “elevated,” suggesting some sort of qualitative change in the assessment of the price situation. This is followed by the acknowledgement that the new assessment is almost completely a result of higher global energy prices.
  • The revisions to the second paragraph are subtle, but they offer some insight into how the Fed’s interpretation of the Middle East conflict has evolved. Previously, the Fed framed developments in the region primarily as a source of uncertainty. Now, the language separates the source of the shock from the uncertainty around its effects. The statement more clearly acknowledges that the conflict is already impacting the global economy, particularly through energy channels, while emphasizing that the ultimate implications for the economic outlook remain highly uncertain.

The April FOMC statement also has the special case of there being a significant decision to NOT change a paragraph, which left the Committee with three hawkish dissents. Yes, this specifically refers to that “easing bias” phrase highlighted above, which suggests cuts are still likely to be the next move. In times when the FOMC seemed to be more in harmony, a decision like this would seem dovish, but in the current environment, it highlights the division among FOMC members and perhaps also sends a message to the White House and the potential new FOMC Chair (Kevin Warsh) that the inflation hawks will not be easily swayed towards supporting rate cuts.

The hawkish FOMC members are not lacking reasons to be steadfast. Key data points since the March FOMC meeting have not been friendly to the case for rate cuts. The March CPI index, the clearest view on inflation since the Middle East conflict began, was up 0.9% MoM and 3.3% YoY, the highest headline annual rate since May 2024, and the core CPI index was up 2.6% YoY, the highest reading so far this year. At the same time, the March jobs report was strong on both the demand (+186k private jobs) and supply (unemployment rate down -0.1 ppt to 4.3%) side. Therefore, the pause today is very much not a surprise, and the hawks showing concern for “easing bias” is also not too surprising either.

Press Conference

Opening Statement

At 2:30 pm, Chair Powell opened what will be his final press conference as Federal Reserve Chair, with his term set to end on May 15, 2026. Over his tenure, he has guided the Committee through a series of turbulent periods, consistently emphasizing the Fed’s independence and its commitment to the dual mandate. His closing statement attempts to strike a careful balance between increasingly divided perspectives: inflation hawks concerned about a renewed surge in price pressures, and a more dovish contingent aligned with the incoming Chair, Kevin Warsh.

  • Powell’s assessment of economic activity has not changed much. He continues to see activity expanding at a “solid pace,” buoyed by the strength in consumer spending and business fixed investment. The housing sector remains a weak point.
  • Powell’s description of the labor market has not changed much either, despite the strong March jobs report. He describes the unemployment rate as “little changed” and job gains as remaining “low.” He also continues to accentuate the two-sided dynamics in the labor market: labor supply falling due to lower immigration and labor demand softening.
  • Powell’s inflation assessment has changed. He describes it as having “moved up recently” and being “elevated relative to our two percent longer run goal.” He points to oil prices as being a factor, but also notes that the Fed estimates March core PCE inflation at around 3.2% YoY, reflecting tariffs. He also admits that inflation expectations have risen in response to higher energy prices, but “most” longer-term measures are consistent with the 2% inflation goal.

In the closing portion of his statement, Powell stepped back from the near-term outlook to emphasize the broader role of the Federal Reserve, highlighting its responsibility to support stable prices, a strong labor market, and a resilient financial system through independent, collective decision-making. He underscored the importance of institutional continuity, expressing confidence in the Fed’s ability to carry out its mission under new leadership while maintaining its commitment to objectivity and public service. Addressing recent legal developments, Powell noted progress toward resolving the investigation surrounding him and reiterated that his decisions will be guided by what best serves the institution. He closed with a clear signal on his own path forward, confirming that after his term as Chair ends on May 15, he will remain on the Board of Governors for a period of time.

Q&A

Following the opening statement, Chair Powell faced the media for the final time as the Chair. The questions offered to him touched on many different topics. Here are a select few that were most interesting:

  • Can you talk about what has gone into your decision to remain on the Board? - The first question dove right into his reasons for remaining on the board. In this answer, he was a little more direct than usual, pointing to the “legal attacks on the Fed” which Powell said, “threaten our ability to conduct monetary policy without considering political factors.” This is one of the most clear-cut responses of his criticizing the efforts by the White House to gain leverage on the Fed. He continues to emphasize the need for the Fed to be able to “conduct monetary policy without taking into consideration political factors.” He also points out that he had planned on retiring following his term as Chair, but he’s “literally staying because of the actions that have been taken.” There is no way to obfuscate that message. Given the way his term is ending, this will probably be what Powell’s legacy as Fed Chair will be defined by: his defense of the Fed’s independence.
  • Can you help us understand how the inflation outlook has changed… before getting to the energy shock…? - Powell answers this in two parts. He addresses tariffs, which are still seen as “a one-time price increase that would go away over time.” He suggests that FOMC members expect that to be happening in the next two quarters, but they are watching carefully. On the energy shock, Powell points out that it is “textbook” to look through a rise in energy prices, so there is no need to react right away. Instead, the Fed needs to be cautious and eventually see the backside of the energy shock before forming a more concrete reaction. He also states that energy “hasn’t even peaked yet.”
  • Why is that easing bias still appropriate, given how different the inflation outlook is now? - Apparently, there has been much discussion on the decision to keep an easing bias, and if it was still appropriate to keep that bias. Powell says that “the number of people on the Committee who either could support that language change… changing to a more neutral stance so that a hike is likely as a cut… that number has increased over the intermeeting period.” Powell stated that he didn’t see a reason to make that change, given that there was a lot of uncertainty surrounding the conflict in the Middle East, but he understood why people would want to (referring to the three dissenters).
  • Is there any sense that interest rates might have to go up? - Nobody supported a rate hike in today’s meeting, and nobody is saying “we need to hike now.” Powell says that “a group of us, including me, didn’t feel like we needed to be in a hurry on that.” In general, his response did not give the impression that there were any members seriously considering a hike, but that the discussion was centered on the forward guidance being tilted towards easing.
  • Do you think we're at the neutral rate, and why or why not? - Powell has been asked versions of this question before, and in previous press conferences, he has given the impression that he thinks we are probably around neutral. Since he’s been a little bit looser in his final press conference, he provides some more color:

I think pretty close to the neutral rate, yeah. I always had it between three and four percent. We're little north of three-and-a-half. So that's well in the range of what I consider reasonable. But at the higher end of the range what I would consider reasonable neutral rate.

  • How do you see the risks of oil prices bleeding into core inflation? - Powell immediately responds, “those prospects are real.” The rest of his response is murkier. He again asserts that he believes that rates are near neutral “or perhaps mildly restrictive,” and that stance means the Fed can “wait here and see how things work out before we act.” He again points to uncertainty as another reason to remain on pause because there are too many scenarios to consider.
  • Does the majority of the Committee still have a bias towards cut? - With the three new dissents, there does seem to be a clear shift in the balance of the FOMC regarding this “easing bias.” Powell cannot deny this. He claims that “the center is moving toward a more neutral place,” which is a nice way of saying that members are becoming more hawkish. He suggests that there could be a shift in signaling if conditions do change, but the key thing is that everyone agreed on what to do (except one).
  • Do you think that Fed independence is as strong as when you became Chair? - Powell states very clearly that “I think it’s at risk,” and he points to this risk as a reason for him staying on the Federal Reserve Board. He finishes saying, “We're all human. Don't expect perfection. But do expect us to make decisions without political considerations and the very best analysis we can bring.”

Market Reaction

It is safe to say that the decision, the dissents, and the statements from the broader Committee and Powell all had hawkish tones to them. The loudest, of course, were the three Federal Reserve Bank presidents who all opted to make official their objections to the statement keeping phrasing that points to a rate cut being the next change in the federal funds rate. This solidified that the inflation outlook had clearly gotten worse and that FOMC members weren’t going to shift their views under new leadership.

  • The market responded with some hawkish moves of its own. After the press statement was released at 2 pm EST, announcing the three dissents, the S&P 500 (SP500) dropped about -0.3%, remaining there until the press conference began. The index recovered through the end of the NY session and was roughly unchanged on the day. The NASDAQ index (COMP:IND) actually ended higher by 0.60%, following a similar pattern, and the DJIA (DJI) was down -0.60%. Overall, equities were relatively resilient in the face of the resolve of the inflation hawks.
  • The bond market had more of a one-directional move. Treasury yields jumped after the release of the press statement and maintained those gains through the end of the press conference. In the end, the yield curve was up across all durations, with strong 10 bps gains in the 2-year, 3-year, and 5-year yields, and an 8 bps rise in the 10-year yield.
  • While this was all happening, oil prices offered more fuel to the hawkish fire. As of 4 pm, the WTI oil price (CL1:COM) was up almost 8.2% to $108, and Brent had risen around 5.8% to just over $110.

The materials resulting from the April FOMC meeting and decision had a clear impact on rate expectations, and it would not be one that the newly appointed Fed Chair would appreciate. CME Group’s FedWatch probabilities now suggest that the market believes a rate hike is more likely to happen in each of the FOMC meetings scheduled between December 2026 and September 2027, a massive shift from the market’s assessment after the March FOMC meeting, when hike probabilities never surfaced above 5%.

The net result of the April meeting is a clear shift in how markets are interpreting the Fed’s path forward. While the Committee itself has not moved rates and continues to maintain language that points to easing as the next step, the combination of firmer inflation data, rising energy prices, and visible internal resistance to that easing bias has pushed expectations in a more hawkish direction. Rate cuts are no longer seen as the default outcome, and for the first time in this cycle, markets are beginning to assign meaningful probability to the possibility that the next move could instead be a hike. In that sense, the Fed may not have changed its stance outright, but the reaction function has become far more two-sided, leaving policy expectations increasingly dependent on how inflation evolves from here. This shift also runs counter to the expectation that new leadership under Warsh would naturally tilt policy in a more dovish direction, reinforcing that the Committee as a whole remains anchored by inflation risks rather than leadership transition.

data.title: Adobe At $240 Reminds Me Of Alphabet At $165
data.description:
Overview

Adobe Inc. (ADBE) is a leading multinational software company best known for industry-standard digital creation tools such as Photoshop, Illustrator, Lightroom, and Acrobat PDF. These tools are used for photo editing, video editing, and production, which are useful for content creation and marketing.

The stock is down more than 60% from its all-time highs and is down 35% in the last year. One major reason for the recent decline is the narrative about how AI could impact its business model and potentially render its creative software subscriptions useless.

I am not on that team. I think the drawdown in Adobe’s stock price presents a very good buying opportunity here. It appears that revenue growth has bottomed out, and the AI tools are being received well. The stock also trades at very compressed valuation multiples, especially relative to its peers and historical averages. It is for those reasons that Adobe’s current position reminds me of where Alphabet was in June-July of last year.

Bullish Triggers

New Offerings Gaining Momentum

With concerns around slowing growth in mind, the company is innovating within its core flagship applications as well as investing in newer offerings, namely, Adobe Express, Adobe Firefly, and Adobe GenStudio. Adobe Express is a cloud-based design and content creation application with AI-powered content creation, most helpful for marketers, small businesses, beginners, and other users who may not have advanced design skills. Adobe Firefly is a set of Gen-AI models that Adobe has integrated within its flagship applications, such as Photoshop and Illustrator, which allow users to generate high-quality photos, videos, etc., from text-based prompts. Lastly, Adobe GenStudio is a gen-AI application designed for enterprise-grade marketers and finds immense application in digital marketing campaigns.

During Q1, the company crossed 850 million MAUs of Acrobat, Creative Cloud, Express, and Firefly, which represents 17% YoY growth. Specifically for Express, MAU tripled YoY, and it is now being used in 99% of the U.S. Fortune 500 companies. The company also mentions that enterprises are looking to Adobe Firefly Enterprise to “unlock a new era of content creation.” Generative credit consumption grew by 45% QoQ (+342% annualized), with video generations growing 8x YoY. This is reflected in Firefly subscription ARR growing 75% QoQ (+837% annualized). New customer acquisitions also grew 50% QoQ for Firefly Enterprise, showcasing strong enterprise adoption.

The growth in new offerings reflects the strong customer response. In my opinion, the adoption of these new offerings by enterprises is a very strong signal because enterprises would not adopt a tool that does not add value to them, and they are also likely to be customers for multiple years, which increases visibility and predictability of future revenue. I believe that the increased adoption of these new offerings also increases the scope of monetizing (and slowly raising prices for) these new offerings within the next 12-15 months, which will be accretive for margins.

This reminds me of the time when Google had begun monetizing AI Overviews within the traditional Search business and the ads on AI Overviews were monetizing at the same rate as the traditional Search business. However, the market had not caught on to this revival of the Search business until the Gemini-3 model brought a spotlight onto Google’s AI capabilities. While I don’t know if or when an immediate catalyst for Adobe will emerge, I strongly feel that the continuation of revenue growth momentum into the next quarter will surely bring more bullish attention to the stock.

Growth Bottomed Out and is Now Accelerating

As can be seen in the graph below, Adobe struggled for growth in 2025 in the low 10s, but during Q1 2026, revenue growth rose to almost 12%. This is the fastest rate of revenue growth since at least Q3 2023 and higher by almost 150 bps compared to the previous quarter. I think this suggests a very clear acceleration in growth and a change in the trend rate of growth for the company. I see this acceleration in revenue growth lasting at least a few quarters and, via operating leverage, creating much faster growth in operating profits.

On the topic of Operating Income, the below graph shows Adobe’s operating income and YoY growth for the same. Adobe’s operating income grew 14.66% in Q1 2026, the fastest in five quarters. The last two quarters show that operating income growth has also rebounded from the lows of Q3 2025.

As the company continues to build new offerings on top of or within existing flagship applications as well as launch AI-first offerings such as Firefly, I expect revenue growth to be safely above the 10-11% range and possibly accelerate into the low teens. The re-acceleration in revenue growth and operating income growth is very encouraging and not priced in by the market, given that the stock is down close to 30% in the last six months/two quarters. In my opinion, this will create room for further upside in the future due to expansion or normalization of valuation multiples.

Valuation – Median Multiples and Historical Averages

Talking of expansion and normalization in valuation multiples, let’s conduct a thorough valuation analysis of Adobe using its historical valuation multiples as benchmarks and using the median multiples of Adobe’s trading peers – Autodesk, Salesforce, Docusign, Figma, Intuit, and Oracle.

The below image shows the publicly listed peers of Adobe. I have used five valuation multiples – EV/Sales, EV/EBIT, P/E, EV/OCF, and EV/FCFF. Based on the median of each valuation multiple, we get a range of implied equity value per share of Adobe between $333.10 from the EV/FCFF and $513.87 from the EV/EBIT. I decided to further take a median of all these five implied equity values to arrive at a single price target. The median of these five was $453.21.

The other valuation method I will use is comparing Adobe’s current valuation to its historical average. I am using data since 2010, which includes the post-GFC bull market, the slowdown into COVID, the sharp rebound after COVID, the 2022 bear market, and the most recent AI-fear-driven drawdown in the stock price and valuation multiples.

The below image shows the current valuation multiple and its percentile from the last 15 years. Then there is the 15-year average valuation multiple and the potential upside/downside if the stock were to reach that multiple. Typically, I would stop here. However, the $674 target price is too high and unlikely to be achieved in the next 6-12 months. Hence, I decided to look at the 25th percentile (lower quartile) valuation multiple over the last 15 years. After calculating the potential upside to the 25th percentile for each valuation ratio, I looked at the median upside to calculate the price target. A potential 90% upside resulted in a price target of $462.57.

Among the two price targets, I feel that the one with trading comparables makes more sense. This is because Adobe’s revenue growth has slowed down considerably compared to pre-COVID levels. For that reason, I am setting a price target of $450 (rounded down from $453) on ADBE.

It did not take Alphabet very long to double from its lows after the market was convinced about its AI capabilities. With how Adobe’s AI-first offerings are being received by enterprises and all other customer groups, it would not surprise me if Adobe reached my price target by the end of this year or maybe even earlier, given that the broader market performance is supportive.

Risks

Growth Slowdown

With the stock trading at a forward P/E of nearly 10, a lot of risks are already priced in. If you ask me, pretty much all risks are priced in and arguably priced in more than they may actually materialize.

After the Q1 2026 earnings, one big risk to my thesis is that other AI tools emerge that will once again pose a headwind to revenue growth for Adobe. I do not see material downside risks to revenue growth in the future. This is because Adobe is already well integrated into enterprise working processes, so new tools will most likely struggle to replace Adobe and its applications. Additionally, Adobe’s new offerings are receiving a very good response and rapid adoption from its existing and new clients. This creates revenue growth tailwinds, as we have seen earlier in the article.

The other way in which new AI tools will impact Adobe is by dampening sentiment around the stock and further compressing valuation multiples; this too is unlikely to occur, in my opinion. The stock already trades at the lowest ever P/E multiples since 2010 and the 12th percentile of P/S multiples since 2010. This means that risk from compression of valuation multiples is very limited, and the risk/reward is strongly in favor of the bulls.

Conclusion

I want to end this article with a Strong Buy rating on Adobe with a Price Target of $450 derived using the Median-Multiple valuation methodology. My opinion is that the risks from AI disrupting Adobe have created an asymmetric opportunity with risks to the upside. The Q1 earnings call shows strong adoption of the company’s new offerings and AI-first tools and applications. This is creating acceleration in the growth rate of revenue. Based on the recent decline in the stock price, I do not think this turnaround is priced in.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch Fed Special: Powell Doesn't Go Gently Into That Good Night
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Jerome Powell will stay as governor. (0:17) FOMC holds rates steady as four members dissent. (1:54) Treasury yields rise, markets see no rate moves this year. (2:37)

The following is an abridged podcast:

Jerome Powell said he will remain on the Federal Reserve Board as a governor until he is satisfied that the Justice Department’s investigation into the Fed is truly resolved.

In his final press conference as chair, Powell struck a firm tone. While welcoming the DOJ’s decision to close the probe, he noted the department also said it could reopen the investigation, leaving him with “no choice but to stay” to safeguard the Fed’s independence.

“I am literally staying because of the actions of the administration,” Powell said, adding that his concerns were not about verbal criticism by elected officials.

President Donald Trump has repeatedly criticized Powell for not cutting rates and recently said he would move to fire him if he stayed on. Powell said his tenure as governor will continue for a period “to be determined.”

“My concern is really about the series of legal attacks on the Fed which threaten our ability to conduct monetary policy without considering political factors,” Powell said.

“These legal actions by the administration are unprecedented,” he added. “I worry these attacks are battering the institution.”

Renaissance Macro said the question now is “whether Trump takes the bait and decides to go after Powell for doing something that is within Powell’s remit,” adding that any attempt to remove him would escalate tensions around Fed independence.

Powell said he intends to keep a low profile when Kevin Warsh takes the helm. The Senate Banking Committee earlier advanced Warsh’s nomination to the full Senate.

Tom Graff, CIO at Facet, said, “Powell can say what he wants about not being a ‘shadow chair,’ but his words are going to carry so much weight with the FOMC. My guess is Powell becomes a stand-in for Fed independence within the FOMC.”

As for the decision itself, the FOMC held rates steady at 3.5%–3.75%, as widely expected. But there were four dissenting votes — the most since 1992.

Steve Miran voted for a rate cut. Beth Hammack, Neel Kashkari and Lorie Logan dissented over what they viewed as an easing bias in the statement, arguing policy language should remain neutral between hikes and cuts.

Macro strategist Dario Perkins said, “The dumb take is that Powell has lost control. The smart take is that this is the FOMC telling Warsh he won’t have control.”

Following the decision and press conference, market-implied odds of rates staying steady through year-end rose to 88%, with pricing showing slightly higher odds of a hike than a cut.

After all that, stocks ended little changed as traders reset for the onslaught of postmarket earnings. The S&P (SP500) and Nasdaq (COMP.IND) finished barely in the red, while the Dow (DJI) lost 0.6%.

The real Fed reaction showed up in the Treasury market, where yields moved higher across the curve.

The 2-year yield (US2Y), most closely tied to the fed funds rate, rose 9 basis points to 3.94%. The benchmark 10-year (US10Y) climbed 6 basis points to 4.42%. The 30-year (US30Y) approached the 5% level, rising 5 basis points to 4.99%.

As Powell stepped away from the lectern for the final time as chair, he closed with a simple line: “Thank you very much everyone. I won't see you next time.”

data.title: F5: EMEA Regulatory Tailwinds, But High Stock-Based Comp Is Worth Considering
data.description:
F5, Inc. (FFIV) reported a strong quarter this week, and the stock has responded accordingly, climbing 7%. At first glance, it probably looks like a straightforward beat-and-raise story, and in many ways it is. But I think there are a few things worth digging into beneath the surface, particularly around what the AI revenue numbers actually mean, whether the European regulatory tailwind has legs, and a valuation question that does not get enough attention in the coverage I have read. I am rating FFIV a ‘hold.’ The business is in good shape, but I think the stock is pricing in a lot of the good news already, and once you adjust for stock-based compensation, the multiple is less attractive than it appears.

A Look At The Latest Quarter

F5 reported a strong quarter for Q2’26 with revenues of $812 million (a beat of $29 million), up 11% compared to last year with product revenue advancing 22% to $411 million on the back of systems revenue up 26% to $226 million. Software revenue clocked in at $184 million, up 17%, of which subscription-based software made up 90% and grew 20%. Services revenue was up slightly by 2% to $401 million, while recurring revenue reached 70% of the total.

When looking at what led to the results this quarter, F5 had pretty strong growth across hybrid multicloud refreshes and expansions, with now 7 consecutive quarters of double-digit product growth underscoring platform momentum. Some of the key highlights included approximately $50 million in direct AI use-case sales in the first half, more than doubling year over year, as the company approached 100 customers deploying F5 solutions for AI data delivery, runtime security, and factory load balancing.

International strength stood out with EMEA revenue up 22% and APAC up 19%, which was helped by digital sovereignty initiatives and competitive displacements, and platform consolidations in verticals like healthcare and energy drove higher-capacity systems wins and footprint expansion during refresh cycles.

Looking at the company’s profitability, gross margins stood at 83.7%, up 60 bps compared to last year. Operating margins were 190 bps higher at 33.8%. This growth was driven by a surge in high-value product sales, particularly in AI-ready hardware and software subscriptions. Again, since 90% of their software revenue now comes from recurring subscriptions, the software means that once it’s built, this directly boosts their gross and operating margins. With the strong operating leverage, EPS of $3.90 was 14% higher compared to last year and was 44 cents above sell-side estimates.

As a software-adjacent business, F5 benefits from operating leverage, but when it comes to financial leverage, it has none. At quarter end, the company had a cash position of $1.46 billion. FCF conversion is also high, as on an operating cash flow of $366 million, FCF came out to $348 million. F5 generates cash upfront through deferred revenue, and we can see that play out on the liabilities section of the balance sheet, up 8.1% to $850 million.

On the earnings call, while management called out $100 million in share repurchase at an average price of $269 million, with FY’26 repurchases expected to be at least 50% of free cash flow, it’s worth pointing out that the share count only decreased by 2%, yet stock-based compensation as a percentage of revenue was 9.3% as a percentage of revenue. With $75.5 million of stock-based compensation during the quarter, almost three-quarters of those share repurchases aren’t actually creating value for shareholders. Rather, they are real expenses for the business and deserve to be deducted from EBITDA when analyzing valuation.

Despite a major breach last year where a China-nexus group using malware was found to have had long-term persistent access to F5's internal BIG-IP development environment, F5’s business has operated mostly without disruption.

One of the more underappreciated parts of the F5 thesis right now is the fact that European cybersecurity regulation is effectively forcing enterprise spending into exactly what F5 sells. NIS2, which became enforceable across EU member states in late 2024, mandates cybersecurity risk management, incident reporting, and operational resilience across a huge swath of "essential" and "important" industries: energy, healthcare, banking, transport, digital infrastructure, and more. DORA, the Digital Operational Resilience Act for financial services, has been fully in force since January 2025 with even more prescriptive requirements.

What this means practically is that enterprises (particularly in Europe) that deferred infrastructure refresh cycles are now under compliance deadlines. Hybrid multi-cloud architectures with consistent security controls across on-premises and cloud environments are no longer optional. They are mandated. F5's BIG-IP and Distributed Cloud Services platform is built for exactly this kind of deployment. CEO Locoh-Donou has been explicit about this tailwind on several of the last few earnings calls.

Germany only completed its NIS2 transposition in December 2025. Enforcement timelines are staggered across member states into 2027. The EU Cyber Resilience Act adds another layer with deadlines running into 2027-2028. To me, the 22% EMEA growth this quarter is not a one-off. More likely, it’s a reflection of a multi-year regulatory spend cycle that disproportionately benefits vendors with deep hybrid infrastructure footprints. And F5 is one of the clearest plays on that theme.

F5 Has A Strong Competitive Position, But Not Without Threats

But F5 isn’t the only name that’s playing in the sandbox on this trend. F5's BIG-IP platform is the enterprise standard for Layer 4-7 load balancing, SSL offload, WAF, and API security in hybrid environments. Enterprises with large, complex BIG-IP deployments don't switch lightly, and the F5’s CFO noted that competitive takeout rates have gone up "materially." My guess is that as hybrid multi-cloud complexity grows, platform consolidation is more attractive than managing a patchwork of point solutions. Companies like F5 that have breadth here likely have an advantage.

That said, I don't think the competitive picture is entirely comfortable. Citrix's NetScaler is still the most direct ADC rival, and Cloud Software Group has been pushing it hard into hybrid architectures. In the cloud-native space, Cloudflare (NET) is the competitor I watch most closely. It bundles content delivery, web application security, DDoS protection, and bot filtering into a single platform at a price point that is hard to argue with. The risk is not that Cloudflare rips out a Fortune 500 company's decade-old F5 deployment. It probably won't. But it doesn't have to in order to quietly win the next generation of enterprise customers before they ever become F5 customers.

The big hyperscalers are a slower-moving threat but still real. They’ve mostly continued to commoditize basic load balancing, pushing F5 further up the value stack. So far this has worked out fine for F5, but it is a dynamic worth monitoring.

What To Expect In Q3 And Beyond

F5 raised its FY’26 revenue growth outlook to 7-8% from the prior 5-6% and guided Q3’26 revenue to a range of $820-840 million, implying roughly 6.5% growth at the midpoint. In my view, this upward revision reflects the strong close rates and pipeline momentum management highlighted, particularly in hybrid multicloud refreshes. For the full year they continue to expect mid-single-digit software growth, double-digit systems growth, and low single-digit services growth, with the gross and operating margin ranges left unchanged at 82.5-83.5% and 34-35%, respectively. I believe the guidance strikes a balanced tone given the visibility they now have into the second half.

For Q3, the company flagged that higher component costs (primarily memory) will cause a sequential step-down in gross margin that’ll last into Q4, though they still see full-year margins holding within the guided band. To me this is a near-term dynamic worth watching, especially as memory pricing remains elevated through much of FY’27, but management seems to be somewhat confident that supply visibility remains solid for the current build plans.

Longer term, management noted that they expect “even stronger software growth next year than this year.” That tells me the current mid-single-digit pace is more of a base-effect story than a demand slowdown. On the systems side, they “do expect there to be a growth opportunity for the systems business” into next year, which I interpret as confirmation that the refresh-plus-expansion dynamic is not a one-year phenomenon. Given these factors and the strong momentum with 8 out of 8 beats in the last few quarters, I like the setup for the balance of the year (source: Bloomberg).

Valuation Isn't Cheap When Adjusting SBC

On valuation, F5 looks reasonable at first glance. On a forward P/E basis, the company trades at 19.3x earnings, which is a below-market multiple for a business that the sell-side has modeled at 17% EPS growth to the end of the decade (source: Bloomberg). But I think when looking at the valuation, it’s important to keep in mind some of the add-backs being made to EBITDA.

On EBITDA, analysts are expecting $946 million in EBITDA, which is consistent with the guidance assumptions from management. However, when looking at the company’s stock-based compensation, it’s increased quite materially in the last few years. According to Bloomberg, stock-based comp has increased from $162.9 million in FY’19 to $231.5 million in FY'25. At the same time, sales per employee have only increased from $421.1k to $469.5k. When deducting the company’s $231.5 million of stock-based comp from EBITDA (since I view this to be a real expense, despite it being non-cash), the true valuation is likely closer to 24.8x EV/EBITDA. To get there, I’m using a $17.7 billion enterprise value against $946 million in EBITDA less $232 million of SBC. So while it’s worth noting that forward EBITDA in FY’27 is materially higher at $1.24 billion, our same EV/(EBITDA less SBC) is still 17.6x, which is high relative to peers.

Bottom Line

F5’s hybrid multi-cloud thesis is real, and the EMEA regulatory tailwind is not going away anytime soon. But at roughly 24.8x EV/EBITDA once you strip out stock-based compensation, I don’t think the stock is cheap enough to get excited about right now. I want to see software growth start to inflect more meaningfully and hear about what management says at the May 28 Investor Day before getting more constructive. For now, I’d be more comfortable sitting on the sidelines.

data.title: SoFi Technologies' Plunge Is A Gift
data.description:
April 29th was a rough day for shareholders of SoFi Technologies, Inc. (SOFI). The stock dropped about 13.6% in afternoon trading after management announced financial results covering the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Objectively speaking, it was actually a good time for the company. Revenue, profits, and cash flows rose nicely. The firm continues to see an increase in its book value per share and tangible book value per share on a year-over-year basis. And guidance points toward additional significant growth throughout this year.

The problem, however, was the fact that the market decided to punish the company for falling short of analysts’ expectations when it came to both top line and bottom line performance. But honestly, I view this as a good opportunity for those who are bullish about the business. Depending on which metric you use, the stock is quite cheap. And when you factor in its continued expansion, it makes sense to be bullish on it right now. That's why I have no problem reaffirming it as a Buy candidate after previously upgrading it to that in March of this year.

A great time meets pessimism

By almost every measure you could look at, the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year was a good time for shareholders of SoFi Technologies. Management reported revenue of $1.10 billion. That happened to be 42.6% above the $771.8 million that the business reported a year earlier. And yet, the market seemed unhappy since revenue did fall short of what analysts anticipated to the tune of about $30 million. This overall expansion that the company saw was thanks to a continued rise in the number of members and products that it has. The company ended 2025 with 13.65 million members. But that number popped to 14.71 million by the first quarter of this year. As the number of members increases, the number of products, which would be the number of features that they utilize that generate revenue for the company, increases as well.
